title: Trump's history on PFAS regulation
description: The first Trump administration made a number of commitments to address PFAS contamination, but ultimately failed to follow through on most of them. The Biden administration finalized many of them. But the second Trump administration is now trying to roll some of them back, despite much of its rhetoric on PFAS.

timeline.title: PFAS National Leadership Summit
timeline.description: The Environmental Protection Agency announces at a summit with federal, state and industry stakeholders a plan to "initiate steps to evaluate the need for a maximum contaminant level (MCL) for PFOA and PFOS" in drinking water. The agency also announces it is "beginning the necessary steps to propose designating PFOA and PFOS as 'hazardous substances'" under Section 102 of the Comprehensive Environmental Response, Compensation, and Liability Act (CERCLA).

timeline.title: PFAS Action Plan commits to holding "responsible parties accountable for PFAS releases into the environment"
timeline.description: Trump EPA outlines a roadmap for addressing PFAS contamination in drinking water, including "steps to evaluate the need for a maximum contaminant level" for PFOA and PFOS. EPA also announces as part of its PFAS Action Plan that it has "initiated the regulatory development process for listing PFOA and PFOS as CERCLA hazardous substances" and says doing so would give the agency "additional authority to address PFOA and PFOS, including the ability to require responsible parties to carry out and/or pay for response actions."

timeline.title: EPA official promises Congress a regulatory determination by end of 2019
timeline.description: EPA Assistant Administrator for the Office of Water David Ross tells the Senate Environment and Public Works Committee that the agency will propose a drinking water regulatory determination for PFOA and PFOS "by the end of this year." He also agrees with the House Subcommittee on Environment that the potentially widespread contamination of America's drinking water with PFAS constitutes a "national emergency."

timeline.title: EPA publicly commits to regulatory determination by end of year
timeline.description: The Environmental Protection Agency puts out a press release reiterating its intent to "issue its proposed regulatory determination for PFOA and PFAS" by the end of 2019, calling it "the next step in the drinking water standard setting process."

timeline.title: PFAS Action Act of 2019 dies in Senate amid White House opposition
timeline.description: The White House — a few weeks after missing its own deadline to propose a regulatory determination for PFOA and PFOS in drinking water — publishes a statement opposing the PFAS Action Act of 2019 on procedural grounds and threatens to veto it. The statement, released just days before Congress was scheduled to vote on the January 2019 bill, acknowledges "the public concern" surrounding PFAS but argues the bill would require the administration to "bypass well-established processes, procedures, and legal requirements," of existing environmental laws, including the Safe Drinking Water Act. The bill passes the House of Representatives by a vote of 247-159, with 24 Republicans voting with most Democrats in favor of the bill, but fails to advance in the Senate.

timeline.title: Trump EPA initiates regulatory process for potential drinking water limits for PFOA and PFOS
timeline.description: The Environmental Protection Agency issues a proposed rule making preliminary determinations to regulate drinking water levels of PFOA and PFOS — the two most well-known "forever chemicals" — under the Safe Drinking Water Act. This action formally initiaties the regulatory process to determine whether to set legally enforceable limits for the two chemicals under the Safe Drinking Water Act.

timeline.title: Trump administration initiates process for potentially designating PFOA and PFOS as hazardous substances
timeline.description: Less than a week before leaving office, the first Trump administration puts out a pre-publication advance notice of proposed rulemaking (ANPRM) seeking public comment on whether to designate PFOA and PFOS as hazardous substances under CERCLA and the Resource Conservation and Recovery Act (RCRA). The last-minute action begins the formal process for designating the chemicals as hazardous substances — but leaves the actual designation to the incoming administration.

timeline.title: Trump EPA administrator issues ANPRM on potential PFAS discharge limits for chemical plants
timeline.description: One day after issuing an ANPRM on the potential designation of certain PFAS as regulated hazardous substances under CERCLA, Trump's EPA signs an early version of another ANPRM on potential Clean Water Act regulations limiting the amount of PFAS that manufacturers and formulators can discharge into wastewater. This would restrict the amount of PFAS that can be released into the environment to begin with, as opposed to standards under the Safe Drinking Water Act, which would limit the amount of PFAS drinking water can contain by the time it reaches consumers. Like the other ANPRM, this action is initiated in the final days of the Trump administration and leaves the actual rulemaking to the incoming administration.

timeline.title: Biden freezes all Trump-era regulatory actions
timeline.description: On his first day in office, President Biden's chief of staff, Ron Klain, issues a memo directing federal agencies to freeze all Trump-era regulatory actions that have not yet been finalized pending review. This order covers the Trump EPA's two last-minute ANPRMs on potential PFAS regulations, though the Biden EPA later re-initiates the rulemaking processes for both actions.

timeline.title: Biden EPA finalizes Trump-era proposal to regulate PFOA and PFOS in drinking water
timeline.description: The EPA finalizes the Trump administration's March 2020 preliminary determination to regulate PFOA and PFOS in drinking water under the Safe Drinking Water Act. Unlike the Trump EPA's action, which began the process for determining whether to regulate the chemicals, the Biden EPA's action initiates the process for actually setting drinking water limits for them.

timeline.title: EPA unfreezes rulemaking process for potential PFAS discharge limits
timeline.description: Biden's EPA re-initiates the rulemaking process for potential Clean Water Act regulations that would limit the amount of PFAS manufacturers and formulators can discharge into wastewater.

timeline.title: Biden administration releases PFAS Strategic Roadmap
timeline.description: The Biden administration publishes a comprehensive roadmap for addressing PFAS contamination, which outlines plans to finalize PFAS regulations under the SDWA, CERCLA and others.

timeline.title: Biden EPA proposes designating PFOA and PFOS as CERCLA hazardous substances
timeline.description: After freezing the Trump-era ANPRM, the Biden EPA issues a proposed rule to designate PFOA and PFOS as hazardous substances under Section 102 of CERCLA. If finalized, the designation would set a mandatory reporting threshold for accidental releases of one pound or more of PFOA and PFOS, and require them to be reported within 24 hours of the release. The rule would also give the EPA the authority to hold responsible parties accountable for cleanup and recovery costs.

timeline.title: Biden EPA proposes drinking water standards for six PFAS compounds
timeline.description: EPA publishes the proposed PFAS National Primary Drinking Water Regulation, which includes legally enforceable maximum contaminant levels, or MCLS, for six PFAS compounds: PFOA, PFOS, HFPO-DA (a.k.a., GenX), PFHxS, PFNA and PFBS. The proposed limits for the two most well-known PFAS compounds, PFOA and PFOS, are set at 4 parts per trillion — the lowest reliably detectable amount and the equivalent of one drop of water in five Olympic-sized swimming pools. For context, EPA data show PFOA or PFOS at levels above 4 parts per trillion in more than 16% of water systems tested between 2023 and 2025. The EPA estimates the rule will prevent "thousands of premature deaths, tens of thousands of serious illnesses, including certain cancers and liver and heart impacts in adults, and immune and developmental impacts to infants and children."

timeline.title: Biden EPA considers potential hazardous substance designation for additional PFAS compounds
timeline.description: Biden EPA issues an advance notice of proposed rulemaking (ANPRM) seeking public input on whether to designate additional PFAS compounds — that is, other than PFOA and PFOS — as CERCLA hazardous substances. The ANPRM identifies seven PFAS compounds that were selected "based on the availability of toxicity information"

timeline.title: Biden EPA finalizes first-ever binding drinking water limits for six PFAS compounds
timeline.description: Biden administration enacts legally enforceable drinking water limits — called maximum contaminant levels, or MCLs — for six PFAS compounds. This included defined limits for five individual PFAS compounds, including PFOA and PFOS, as well as a hazard index for mixtures of certain PFAS compounds. The EPA estimates the rule "will prevent thousands of deaths and reduce tens of thousands of serious PFAS-attributable illnesses." Per the rule's compliance deadlines, water systems must meet sampling requirements by April 2027 and must reduce PFAS levels in their drinking water to at or below the maximum contaminant levels by April 2029.

timeline.title: Biden EPA finalizes CERCLA hazardous substance designation for PFOA and PFOS
timeline.description: EPA publishes the final rule designating PFOA and PFOS as CERCLA hazardous substances, effective July 8, 2024. The designation triggers mandatory release reporting at one pound or more and gives EPA authority to pursue cost recovery from polluters at contaminated sites.

timeline.title: Trump administration freezes Biden-era regulatory actions pending review
timeline.description: Trump, like Biden, immediately halts all pending regulatory actions, the result of which is the withdrawal of the March 2021 ANPRM on PFAS discharge limits - a rulemaking process that the first Trump administration initiated.

timeline.title: Trump White House revokes Clinton-era executive order on environmental justice
timeline.description: One day after taking office, Trump revokes Executive Order 12898, a 1994 directive requiring federal agencies to address environmental harms resulting from their actions that disproportionately affected minority and low-income communities. The Biden administration had previously leaned on this directive to factor environmental justice considerations into its PFAS regulatory efforts.

timeline.title: D.C. Circuit grants motion to delay proceedings in AWWA v. EPA by 60 days
timeline.description: Trump's EPA, citing the change of administration and need for more time to familiarize itself with the case, is granted a request to delay litigation challenging the PFAS drinking water rule until early April.

timeline.title: Trump II EPA administrator promises PFAS discharge limits while signaling future rollbacks to drinking water standards
timeline.description: EPA Administrator Lee Zeldin outlines a series of upcoming EPA actions "to combat PFAS contamination," including a commitment to developing discharge standards for PFAS manufacturers — the same action already initiated under the first Trump administration but withdrawn in January 2025 as part of the government-wide regulatory freeze. Zeldin also highlighted upcoming agency action to address "the most significant compliance challenges and requests from Congress and drinking water systems related to national primary drinking water regulations for certain PFAS." This language points to potential rollbacks of the Biden-era PFAS drinking water standards amid industry and political pushback.

timeline.title: D.C. Circuit grants 30-day extension of delay in AWWA v. EPA
timeline.description: Trump administration granted second request to further delay litigation in AWWA v. EPA until mid May.

timeline.title: D.C. Circuit grants 21-day extension of delay in AWWA v. EPA
timeline.description: Trump administration granted third request to delay proceedings in AWWA v. EPA until early June.

timeline.title: EPA announces plan to keep two PFAS drinking water standards, roll back four others
timeline.description: EPA Administrator Lee Zeldin announces the agency's intention to keep the Biden-era maximum contaminant levels for PFOA and PFOS but extend the compliance deadline from 2029 to 2031. But, as Zeldin's March 2025 statement had foreshadowed, EPA simultaneously announces its intent to rescind the regulations and reconsider the regulatory determinations for four other PFAS compounds.

timeline.title: D.C. Circuit grants 45-day extension of delay in AWWA v. EPA
timeline.description: Trump administration granted fourth request to delay proceedings in AWWA v. EPA until the end of July.

timeline.title: EPA files to reverse four of six PFAS drinking water standards
timeline.description: Trump's EPA, following through on its commitment to roll back some of the Biden-era drinking water regulations, files a motion in AWWA v. EPA asking the D.C. Circuit to partially vacate its April 2024 PFAS rule. The agency's motion seeks to rescind the standards for four of the six PFAS compounds - GenX (HFPO-DA), PFHxS, PFNA, and PFBS - while retaining the standards for PFOA and PFOS. The agency says it had "initially attempted to defend the Rule against forceful legal challenges" but upon review concluded that "parts of the rulemaking process were unlawful and parts of the Rule are thus invalid."

timeline.title: Trump II EPA announces it will retain PFOA and PFOS CERCLA designation
timeline.description: EPA Administrator Lee Zeldin criticizes the Biden administration for taking steps to designate PFAS as hazardous substances "without first establishing the rules of the road" and says EPA will outline a "Framework Rule" to clarify how the agency will implement the CERCLA designations and enforce liability going forward. But Zeldin - a founding member of the PFAS Congressional Taskforce and a 'strong supporter of the PFAS Action Act' - also announces the Trump administration will keep the Biden-era CERCLA designations for PFOA and PFOS and defend them in court.

timeline.title: Government shutdown further delays proceedings in AWWA v. EPA
timeline.description: D.C. Circuit delays litigation in AWWA v. EPA amid lapse in appropriations caused by the 43-day government shutdown lasting from Oct. 1 to Nov. 12.

timeline.title: D.C. Circuit denies EPA's motion to partially vacate PFAS rule
timeline.description: Court keeps EPA's PFAS drinking water rule in place while litigation in AWWA v. EPA moves forward.

timeline.title: EPA asks D.C. Circuit to sever PFOA and PFOS drinking water standards in AWWA v. EPA
timeline.description: EPA looks to stall litigation on the drinking water standards for the four PFAS standards it plans to roll back. This would pave the way for the agency to continue defending the two PFAS drinking water standards it intends to uphold while pursuing a separate rulemaking to roll back the others.

timeline.title: D.C. Circuit rejects EPA's motion to sever litigation in AWWA v. EPA
timeline.description: Court ruling prevents EPA from bowing out of litigation over the four PFAS standards it is separately working to rescind.

timeline.title: EPA launches PFAS OUTreach initiative
timeline.description: Trump EPA announces program designed to connect ~3,000 PFOA/PFOS-contaminated water systems with compliance resources and funding ahead of the 2031 deadline.

timeline.title: EPA lists PFAS as priority group, signals possible expansion of drinking water regulations
timeline.description: EPA and HHS list PFAS on a draft list of substances to potentially consider regulating in drinking water. The list excludes the six PFAS compounds already covered by the 2024 rule, pointing to a future increase in PFAS regulations.
